name: Glutathione synthesis and redox cycling
summary: Two-step ATP-dependent synthesis of the tripeptide thiol glutathione (γ-Glu-Cys-Gly) and the NADPH-driven redox cycle by which it detoxifies peroxides, electrophiles and methylglyoxal and buffers cellular thiol/redox state.
description: Glutathione (GSH) is the most abundant low-molecular-weight thiol in animal cells (1–10 mM) and the master intracellular antioxidant and redox buffer. It is a γ-glutamyl tripeptide, L-γ-glutamyl-L-cysteinyl-glycine; the unusual γ-carboxyl peptide bond makes it resistant to normal peptidases and cleavable only by γ-glutamyltransferase. **Synthesis** occurs in the cytosol in two ATP-dependent steps: glutamate–cysteine ligase (GCL) condenses glutamate and cysteine to γ-glutamylcysteine, and glutathione synthetase (GSS) adds glycine. GCL is the rate-limiting, committed step — it is feedback-inhibited by GSH itself and its capacity is set by cysteine availability and by transcriptional induction of its catalytic (GCLC) and modifier (GCLM) subunits. **Redox cycling** underlies its antioxidant function: glutathione peroxidases (the selenoproteins GPX1 for H2O2/soluble hydroperoxides and GPX4 for membrane phospholipid hydroperoxides) oxidize two GSH to the disulfide GSSG while reducing the peroxide to water/alcohol; glutathione reductase (GSR) then regenerates 2 GSH from GSSG at the expense of NADPH. Because that NADPH is supplied chiefly by the oxidative pentose phosphate pathway, the two modules together form the principal cellular defense against oxidative stress — a dependence that makes erythrocytes (which lack mitochondria and de novo synthesis of NADPH by other routes) exquisitely sensitive to G6PD deficiency. **Detoxification** branches use GSH as a nucleophile: glutathione S-transferases (GSTs) conjugate GSH to electrophilic xenobiotics and lipid-peroxidation aldehydes (phase II metabolism), the conjugates being exported and processed to mercapturic acids; and the glutathione-dependent glyoxalase system (glyoxalase I + II) detoxifies methylglyoxal, a reactive 2-oxoaldehyde byproduct of glycolysis, to D-lactate while regenerating GSH. **Turnover and salvage** proceed through the γ-glutamyl cycle: the cell-surface ectoenzyme γ-glutamyltransferase (GGT) initiates breakdown of extracellular GSH, salvaging cysteine — the scarcest precursor. The whole system is coordinated by the KEAP1–NRF2 axis: KEAP1 is a redox sensor that normally targets the transcription factor NRF2 for degradation, and oxidative/electrophilic stress stabilizes NRF2 so it induces GCLC, GCLM, GSR and other antioxidant-response genes. Loss of GPX4-mediated protection drives ferroptosis, an iron-dependent lipid-peroxidation cell death exploited therapeutically in cancer; inherited defects (GSS deficiency causing 5-oxoprolinuria, GCLC deficiency, GGT deficiency) and pharmacologic GSH depletion (buthionine sulfoximine) underline the pathway's clinical importance.

sources.id: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8e, Ch. 22 (glutathione) & Ch. 19 (redox)

references.title: Glutathione metabolism and its implications for health
Keap1 represses nuclear activation of antioxidant responsive elements by Nrf2 through binding to the amino-terminal Neh2 domain
Regulation of ferroptotic cancer cell death by GPX4

compartments.name: Cytosol
compartments.description: Principal site of glutathione synthesis (GCL, GSS) and of redox cycling and detoxification (GPX1, GSR, GSTP1, glyoxalase I/II).

compartments.name: Mitochondrial matrix
compartments.description: Holds a distinct GSH pool; glutathione reductase (GSR) and glyoxalase II (HAGH) are dual-localized to cytosol and mitochondria, and GPX4 protects mitochondrial membranes.

compartments.name: Extracellular space / cell surface
compartments.description: Outer face of the plasma membrane where the ectoenzyme γ-glutamyltransferase (GGT1) degrades extracellular glutathione for cysteine salvage.

compartments.name: Nucleus
compartments.description: Where stabilized NRF2 binds antioxidant-response elements to induce glutathione-metabolism genes.

metabolites.name: L-Glutamate
metabolites.description: Amino-group donor and first substrate of glutamate–cysteine ligase; also the product released when GGT degrades glutathione.

metabolites.name: L-Cysteine
metabolites.description: Thiol-bearing, rate-limiting precursor of glutathione; its intracellular supply (from diet and transsulfuration) sets the ceiling on GSH synthesis.

metabolites.name: Glycine
metabolites.description: Third residue of glutathione, added by glutathione synthetase.

metabolites.name: γ-L-Glutamyl-L-cysteine
metabolites.description: Committed intermediate of glutathione synthesis formed by the rate-limiting glutamate–cysteine ligase.

metabolites.name: Glutathione (reduced, GSH)
metabolites.description: The dominant cellular thiol antioxidant and redox buffer (1–10 mM); electron donor for peroxidases and nucleophile for conjugation; feedback-inhibits its own synthesis at GCL.

metabolites.name: Glutathione disulfide (oxidized, GSSG)
metabolites.description: The two-electron-oxidized dimer of glutathione; the cellular GSH:GSSG ratio (typically >100:1) is a key redox indicator. Regenerated to GSH by NADPH-dependent glutathione reductase.

metabolites.name: NADPH
metabolites.description: Reducing cofactor consumed by glutathione reductase to regenerate GSH; supplied mainly by the oxidative pentose phosphate pathway.

metabolites.name: Hydrogen peroxide
metabolites.description: A reactive oxygen species and redox signaling molecule; reduced to water by glutathione peroxidase 1.

metabolites.name: Lipid hydroperoxide
metabolites.description: Generic peroxidized membrane lipid (KEGG generic compound); the physiological substrate class of GPX4 includes phospholipid, cholesterol and fatty-acyl hydroperoxides whose accumulation drives ferroptosis.

metabolites.name: Lipid (reduced)
metabolites.description: The reduced (hydroxy) lipid product of GPX4 (KEGG generic compound C01356).

metabolites.name: Methylglyoxal
metabolites.description: Reactive dicarbonyl formed largely as a byproduct of glycolysis (from triose phosphates); glycates proteins/DNA and is detoxified by the glutathione-dependent glyoxalase system.

metabolites.name: (R)-S-Lactoylglutathione
metabolites.description: Thioester intermediate of the glyoxalase pathway formed by glyoxalase I from the GSH–methylglyoxal hemithioacetal.

metabolites.name: (R)-Lactate (D-lactate)
metabolites.description: End product of methylglyoxal detoxification released by glyoxalase II with regeneration of GSH.

metabolites.name: L-Cysteinylglycine
metabolites.description: Dipeptide released with glutamate when GGT cleaves glutathione; further hydrolyzed by dipeptidases to salvage cysteine and glycine.

metabolites.name: R-X (electrophilic substrate)
metabolites.description: Generic electrophilic xenobiotic (KEGG generic compound); representative physiological substrates include epoxides, quinones, aryl halides and lipid-peroxidation aldehydes such as 4-hydroxynonenal.

metabolites.name: Halide (HX)
metabolites.description: Leaving group released during glutathione S-conjugation (KEGG generic compound).

metabolites.name: R-S-Glutathione (glutathione S-conjugate)
metabolites.description: Glutathione conjugate of an electrophile (KEGG generic compound); exported by MRP transporters and processed through the mercapturic-acid pathway for excretion.

enzymes.name: Glutamate–cysteine ligase, catalytic subunit (GCLC)
enzymes.description: Catalytic (heavy) subunit carrying the active site; performs the rate-limiting, GSH-feedback-controlled first step of glutathione synthesis. Assembles with the GCLM modifier subunit into the holoenzyme.

enzymes.name: Glutamate–cysteine ligase, modifier subunit (GCLM)
enzymes.description: Regulatory (light) subunit with no catalytic activity of its own; heterodimerization lowers the Km of GCLC for glutamate and raises its Ki for GSH, greatly increasing holoenzyme efficiency. An NRF2/ARE target.

enzymes.name: Glutathione synthetase (GSS)
enzymes.description: Adds glycine to γ-glutamylcysteine in the second, non-rate-limiting ATP-dependent step. Inherited deficiency causes 5-oxoprolinuria (pyroglutamic aciduria).

enzymes.name: Glutathione peroxidase 1 (GPX1)
enzymes.description: Abundant cytosolic/mitochondrial selenoprotein that reduces hydrogen peroxide and soluble organic hydroperoxides to water/alcohol using two GSH; a major peroxide scavenger in erythrocytes.

enzymes.name: Phospholipid-hydroperoxide glutathione peroxidase (GPX4)
enzymes.description: Monomeric selenoprotein uniquely able to reduce hydroperoxides of complex membrane lipids in situ; the essential guardian against lipid peroxidation, and the key negative regulator of ferroptosis.

enzymes.name: Glutathione reductase (GSR/GR)
enzymes.description: FAD-containing, NADPH-dependent homodimer (cytosolic and mitochondrial) that regenerates 2 GSH from GSSG, keeping the glutathione pool reduced. Couples redox defense to NADPH supply from the pentose phosphate pathway.

enzymes.name: Glutathione S-transferase P (GSTP1)
enzymes.description: Representative cytosolic (pi-class) glutathione S-transferase catalyzing phase II conjugation of GSH to electrophiles; also restrains JNK signaling. Frequently overexpressed in tumors, contributing to chemoresistance.

enzymes.name: Glyoxalase I / lactoylglutathione lyase (GLO1)
enzymes.description: Zn2+ metalloenzyme that isomerizes the spontaneously formed GSH–methylglyoxal hemithioacetal to S-D-lactoylglutathione, the first (committed) step of methylglyoxal detoxification.

enzymes.name: Glyoxalase II / hydroxyacylglutathione hydrolase (HAGH)
enzymes.description: Binuclear-zinc thioesterase (cytosolic and mitochondrial) that hydrolyzes S-D-lactoylglutathione to D-lactate, regenerating the GSH consumed by glyoxalase I.

enzymes.name: γ-Glutamyltransferase 1 / glutathione hydrolase (GGT1)
enzymes.description: Cell-surface ectoenzyme (active site facing the extracellular space) that initiates catabolism of extracellular glutathione and GSH conjugates in the γ-glutamyl cycle, enabling cysteine salvage. Serum GGT is a clinical marker of hepatobiliary disease.

enzymes.name: Nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (NRF2)
enzymes.description: Master antioxidant-response transcription factor (not a metabolic enzyme). Represented here as a protein regulator node; on stabilization it dimerizes with small Maf proteins and induces glutathione-synthesis and recycling genes (GCLC, GCLM, GSR and others) through antioxidant/electrophile response elements.

enzymes.name: Kelch-like ECH-associated protein 1 (KEAP1)
enzymes.description: Cytosolic redox/electrophile sensor and CUL3–RBX1 E3-ubiquitin-ligase substrate adaptor (not a metabolic enzyme). Represented here as a protein regulator node; under basal conditions it targets NRF2 for proteasomal degradation, and its cysteine modification by oxidants/electrophiles releases NRF2.

complexes.name: Glutamate–cysteine ligase holoenzyme (GCL)
complexes.description: Heterodimer of the catalytic GCLC and modifier GCLM subunits; the rate-limiting enzyme of glutathione biosynthesis and the target of GSH feedback inhibition. GCLM association tunes GCLC kinetics rather than adding catalytic sites.

genes.name: Glutamate-cysteine ligase catalytic subunit gene
genes.description: Encodes the rate-limiting GCL catalytic subunit; an NRF2/ARE-inducible antioxidant-response gene.

genes.name: Glutamate-cysteine ligase modifier subunit gene
genes.description: Encodes the GCL modifier subunit; an NRF2/ARE target whose induction raises glutathione synthetic capacity.

genes.name: Glutathione reductase gene
genes.description: Encodes glutathione reductase; an NRF2/ARE target coordinating GSSG-recycling capacity with GSH synthesis.

reactions.name: Glutamate–cysteine ligase (committed step)
reactions.equation: L-Glutamate + L-Cysteine + ATP -> γ-L-Glutamyl-L-cysteine + ADP + Pi

reactions.name: Glutathione synthetase
reactions.equation: γ-L-Glutamyl-L-cysteine + Glycine + ATP -> Glutathione + ADP + Pi

reactions.name: Glutathione peroxidase (hydrogen peroxide reduction)
reactions.equation: 2 Glutathione + Hydrogen peroxide -> Glutathione disulfide + 2 H2O

reactions.name: Phospholipid-hydroperoxide glutathione peroxidase (lipid hydroperoxide reduction)
reactions.equation: 2 Glutathione + Lipid hydroperoxide -> Glutathione disulfide + Lipid + 2 H2O

provenance.notes: Substrate/product are the KEGG generic 'lipid hydroperoxide' (C01025) and 'lipid' (C01356). GPX4 physiologically reduces phospholipid, cholesterol and fatty-acyl hydroperoxides within membranes; its inactivation triggers ferroptosis.

reactions.name: Glutathione reductase (GSSG recycling)
reactions.equation: Glutathione disulfide + NADPH + H+ -> 2 Glutathione + NADP+

provenance.notes: Written in the physiological GSH-regenerating direction; KEGG R00115 lists the equation in the opposite (oxidation) orientation. The reaction is effectively unidirectional in vivo because it is driven by NADPH.

KEGG generic GST template (R03522): R-X denotes a generic electrophilic substrate (C01322) and R-S-glutathione the conjugate (C02320). Physiological substrates and specific GST isozymes vary; the conjugate is processed onward to a mercapturic acid.

The true substrate is the hemithioacetal formed non-enzymatically from methylglyoxal and GSH. Written in the physiological detoxifying direction; KEGG R02530 lists the reverse (lyase) orientation. GSH is not consumed net — it is released again by glyoxalase II.

GGT1 is a cell-surface ectoenzyme. Physiologically it transfers the γ-glutamyl group to amino-acid acceptors (transpeptidation, EC 2.3.2.2); the net hydrolytic outcome shown here (EC 3.4.19.13, KEGG R00494) initiates extracellular glutathione turnover and cysteine salvage. Cysteinylglycine is then split by dipeptidases.

regulations.mechanism: Reduced glutathione is the physiological feedback inhibitor of glutamate–cysteine ligase, binding competitively with respect to glutamate (Ki ≈ 1.5–2 mM, near the resting cellular GSH concentration). This non-allosteric product feedback holds synthesis in check and is relieved when GSH falls during oxidative or electrophilic stress. The GCLM modifier subunit raises the Ki, tuning the set-point.

Under basal conditions KEAP1, as the substrate adaptor of a CUL3–RBX1 E3 ubiquitin ligase, binds the Neh2 domain of NRF2 and targets it for constitutive proteasomal degradation, keeping NRF2 low. Oxidants and electrophiles (and inducers such as sulforaphane and dimethyl fumarate) modify reactive KEAP1 cysteines, halting degradation so newly synthesized NRF2 accumulates and enters the nucleus.

Stabilized NRF2 dimerizes with small Maf proteins and binds the antioxidant/electrophile response element (ARE) in the GCLC promoter, inducing the rate-limiting catalytic subunit of glutamate–cysteine ligase and raising glutathione synthetic capacity during oxidative stress.

NRF2 likewise induces the GCL modifier subunit (GCLM) through an ARE; rising GCLM drives formation of the more efficient GCL holoenzyme (lower Km for glutamate, higher Ki for GSH), amplifying the synthetic response.
NRF2 induces glutathione reductase (GSR) through an ARE, matching increased GSSG-recycling capacity to increased GSH synthesis so the reduced glutathione pool is defended under oxidative load.

relations.note: GCLC is the catalytic subunit of the glutamate–cysteine ligase holoenzyme.
GCLM is the modifier subunit that tunes GCLC kinetics within the GCL holoenzyme.
GCLC gene encodes the glutamate–cysteine ligase catalytic subunit.
GCLM gene encodes the glutamate–cysteine ligase modifier subunit.
Paralogous selenium-dependent glutathione peroxidases: GPX1 acts on H2O2 and soluble hydroperoxides, whereas GPX4 uniquely reduces membrane phospholipid hydroperoxides.
NADPH from the oxidative pentose phosphate pathway is the reductant glutathione reductase uses to regenerate GSH; erythrocytes depend on this link, explaining hemolysis in G6PD deficiency.
Glutamate (and glycine) precursors for glutathione synthesis are drawn from amino-acid metabolism; glutamate is also released back to that pool when GGT degrades glutathione.
Cysteine, the rate-limiting precursor of glutathione, is supplied largely by the transsulfuration pathway (from methionine/homocysteine) and salvaged from GSH turnover.
Methylglyoxal is a toxic dicarbonyl formed from the glycolytic triose phosphates (DHAP/glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate); the glutathione-dependent glyoxalase system is its principal detoxification route.
Electron leak from the mitochondrial respiratory chain generates superoxide/H2O2; the glutathione peroxidase–reductase couple is a primary scavenger of this reactive oxygen species.
GSH-dependent GPX4 suppresses lethal accumulation of lipid hydroperoxides; GSH depletion or GPX4 inhibition triggers ferroptosis, a targetable vulnerability in several cancers.
Glutathione S-conjugates are exported and processed (GGT, dipeptidase, N-acetyltransferase) into mercapturic acids for urinary excretion, completing phase II/III detoxification.
